entry.title: Appveyor
entry.description: Appveyor provides cloud-based continuous integration and deployment for Windows, Linux, and macOS projects.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier for public repositories and limited private projects.
caveats: No parallel jobs on free tier

useCases: Automated testing for open source projects
quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Appveyor account.
Connect your GitHub, Bitbucket, or GitLab repository.
Add an appveyor.yml configuration file to your project.
Trigger a build by pushing code to your repository.
Review build results and artifacts in the Appveyor dashboard.
entry.whenToUse: Use Appveyor for CI/CD pipelines, especially if you need Windows build environments or support for open source projects.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require multiple concurrent builds for private projects or advanced deployment features without upgrading.
entry.body: Appveyor offers free continuous integration for public repositories and limited private builds, making it ideal for open source and Windows-centric projects.

entry.title: Astro
entry.description: Astro is a modern static site builder for fast, content-focused websites using minimal JavaScript.

freeTierDetails.summary: Astro is open source and free to use for all projects.
caveats: Hosting is not included; deploy separately

quickstartSteps: Install Node.js (v18+ recommended).
Run 'npm create astro@latest' to scaffold a new project.
Navigate to the project directory and run 'npm install'.
Start the development server with 'npm run dev'.
Build your site with 'npm run build' for production.
entry.whenToUse: Use Astro when you need a fast, SEO-friendly static site with minimal client-side JavaScript.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Astro if you require dynamic server-side rendering or complex backend integrations out of the box.
entry.body: Astro is an open source static site generator designed for building fast, content-rich websites with minimal JavaScript by default. It is ideal for blogs, documentation, and marketing sites, and integrates easily with popular frameworks and deployment platforms. Hosting is not included; you must deploy your built site separately.

entry.title: Azure DevOps
entry.description: Azure DevOps provides cloud-based tools for CI/CD, code repositories, agile planning, and collaboration for software development teams.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes up to 5 users with unlimited private Git repos, 1 Microsoft-hosted CI/CD parallel job, and basic project management.
caveats: Self-hosted agents are limited to 1 parallel job
billingRiskNotes: Adding more than 5 users or exceeding build minutes incurs charges

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Azure account and access Azure DevOps.
Create a new DevOps organization and project.
Import or create a Git repository.
Set up a CI/CD pipeline using Azure Pipelines.
Invite up to 4 additional users to collaborate.
entry.whenToUse: Use Azure DevOps when you need integrated CI/CD, code hosting, and agile planning for small to medium teams.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need unlimited free users, advanced enterprise features without cost, or prefer open-source/self-hosted DevOps tools.
avoidIf: large organizations needing unlimited free users
entry.body: Azure DevOps offers a comprehensive suite for code hosting, CI/CD, and agile project management, ideal for teams seeking integrated cloud-based DevOps workflows.

entry.title: Buildkite
entry.description: Buildkite is a CI/CD platform for running fast, scalable pipelines on your own infrastructure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier for open source projects and a 30-day trial for private projects.
caveats: Private projects require payment after trial

useCases: Automated testing for open source projects
Custom CI/CD pipelines on self-hosted agents
Integrating with GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket
quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Buildkite account.
Connect your code repository (GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket).
Install and configure a Buildkite agent on your infrastructure.
Create a pipeline and add your build steps.
Trigger your first build to verify setup.
entry.whenToUse: Use Buildkite if you need scalable CI/CD pipelines with control over your build infrastructure and strong support for open source projects.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Buildkite if you require fully managed build agents or need a free tier for private, non-open-source projects.
avoidIf: you want free private project builds
entry.body: Buildkite provides a flexible CI/CD platform that lets you run pipelines on your own infrastructure, ideal for teams needing control and scalability.

entry.title: Cloud Build
entry.description: Google Cloud Build is a fully managed CI/CD platform for building, testing, and deploying code at scale.

freeTierDetails.summary: 120 build-minutes per day free with up to 10 concurrent builds.
caveats: Premium machine types and additional minutes incur charges
Storage and network usage may incur costs
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if you exceed free build minutes or use premium features

quickstartSteps: Sign in to Google Cloud and create a project.
Enable the Cloud Build API for your project.
Install and configure the Google Cloud SDK.
Create a cloudbuild.yaml file to define your build steps.
Trigger a build via the Cloud Console or gcloud CLI.
entry.whenToUse: Use Cloud Build for automated CI/CD pipelines, especially if you already use Google Cloud services or need scalable build automation.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require unlimited build minutes, advanced custom runners, or need on-premises build infrastructure.
entry.body: Cloud Build provides managed CI/CD pipelines with a generous free tier, supporting container builds, testing, and deployments tightly integrated with Google Cloud.

entry.title: CodeBuild
entry.description: AWS CodeBuild is a fully managed continuous integration service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces software packages.

freeTierDetails.summary: 100 build minutes per month on the general1.small instance type.
caveats: Charges apply after free minutes are used
billingRiskNotes: Usage beyond free tier incurs charges

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the AWS Management Console.
Navigate to CodeBuild and create a new build project.
Connect your source repository (e.g., GitHub, CodeCommit).
Configure build environment and specify buildspec.yml.
Start a build and monitor the results.
entry.whenToUse: Use CodeBuild when you need managed CI/CD builds for AWS-integrated projects or want to automate testing and packaging.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require unlimited free builds, need advanced build matrix features, or want to avoid AWS account setup and billing risk.
entry.body: AWS CodeBuild offers a managed CI/CD service with a free tier suitable for small workloads, integrating seamlessly with other AWS developer tools.

entry.title: CodeCommit
entry.description: AWS CodeCommit is a fully managed source control service for hosting secure Git repositories.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free for up to 5 active users per month with unlimited repositories and storage.
caveats: Charges apply for additional users or requests.
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding user or request limits incurs charges.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the AWS Management Console.
Navigate to CodeCommit and create a new repository.
Configure Git credentials or AWS CLI access.
Clone the repository to your local machine.
Push your code to the CodeCommit repository.
entry.whenToUse: Use CodeCommit when you need secure, managed Git repositories integrated with AWS services and IAM for access control.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need public repositories, advanced code review tools, or want to avoid AWS account and billing complexities.
entry.body: AWS CodeCommit offers secure, scalable Git repositories with deep AWS integration, suitable for teams needing private source control and seamless AWS workflow support.

entry.title: CodePipeline
entry.description: AWS CodePipeline automates continuous integration and continuous delivery for fast and reliable application and infrastructure updates.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 1 active pipeline per month.
caveats: Some integrations may have separate costs
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if you exceed 1 active pipeline

useCases: Orchestrate multi-stage build and test workflows
quickstartSteps: Sign in to the AWS Management Console.
Navigate to CodePipeline and click 'Create pipeline'.
Configure pipeline settings and select a source provider (e.g., GitHub, CodeCommit).
Add build and deploy stages as needed.
Review and create the pipeline to start automating deployments.
entry.whenToUse: Use CodePipeline to automate CI/CD workflows for applications deployed on AWS or when integrating with other AWS services.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need multi-cloud CI/CD or want to avoid AWS lock-in, or if your team already uses a different CI/CD platform.
entry.body: AWS CodePipeline is a managed CI/CD service that helps automate build, test, and deployment phases for applications and infrastructure on AWS. The free tier allows one active pipeline per month, making it suitable for small teams or projects starting with AWS automation. It integrates natively with other AWS developer tools and supports custom actions, but additional pipelines or integrations may incur charges. CodePipeline is best for AWS-centric workflows and teams looking for a managed, scalable CI/CD solution.

entry.title: Deployment.io
entry.description: Deployment.io provides automated deployment pipelines and hosting for web applications with simple configuration and Git integration.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes limited deployments and basic hosting for small projects.
caveats: No custom domains on free tier

useCases: Automating deployment of static or dynamic web apps
quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Deployment.io account.
Connect your GitHub or GitLab repository.
Configure your deployment pipeline using the web UI.
Deploy your application with a single click.
Monitor deployment status and logs.
entry.whenToUse: Use Deployment.io for quick, automated deployments of small web projects or open source apps with minimal configuration.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need advanced scaling, custom domains, private repo support, or enterprise-grade features.
entry.body: Deployment.io offers a simple, automated way to deploy and host web applications directly from your Git repository, ideal for small projects and open source apps.

entry.title: GitHub Actions
entry.description: GitHub Actions automates workflows for CI/CD, testing, and deployment directly within GitHub repositories.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes limited minutes and storage for public and private repositories.
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free minutes or storage on private repos leads to overage charges

quickstartSteps: Sign in to GitHub and navigate to your repository.
Click the 'Actions' tab and choose a workflow template or set up a new workflow.
Edit the YAML file to define your workflow steps.
Commit the workflow file to the repository.
Push code or trigger the workflow to see it run.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to automate CI/CD, testing, or deployment workflows directly within your GitHub repository.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if your code is not hosted on GitHub or you require unlimited build minutes for private repositories.
avoidIf: large enterprise with custom CI/CD needs
entry.body: GitHub Actions enables developers to automate software workflows, including testing, building, and deploying code, directly from their GitHub repositories.

entry.title: GitLab
entry.description: GitLab offers cloud-based Git repositories, CI/CD pipelines, and DevOps tools for collaborative software development.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes unlimited public/private repositories, 400 CI/CD minutes per month, and basic project management features.
caveats: Additional minutes require paid plan or purchase
Some advanced features require paid tiers
billingRiskNotes: No automatic charges; must upgrade or purchase more minutes

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a free GitLab account at https://gitlab.com.
Create a new project or import an existing repository.
Push your code to the GitLab repository using Git.
Configure a .gitlab-ci.yml file for CI/CD pipelines.
Monitor pipeline runs and manage issues within the project.
entry.whenToUse: Use GitLab when you need integrated Git hosting and CI/CD for collaborative software projects with generous free usage for individuals and small teams.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require unlimited CI/CD minutes, advanced enterprise features, or need to self-host without using GitLab.com.
entry.body: GitLab provides an all-in-one DevOps platform with free private repositories, built-in CI/CD, and project management tools suitable for individuals and small teams.

entry.title: GitLab CI/CD
entry.description: GitLab CI/CD automates code builds, tests, and deployments directly from your GitLab repositories.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 400 CI/CD minutes per month for public and private repositories.
caveats: Free tier may have slower shared runners

useCases: Continuous deployment to staging or production
quickstartSteps: Sign up for a free GitLab account.
Create or import a repository.
Add a .gitlab-ci.yml file to define your pipeline.
Push your code to trigger the pipeline.
Monitor pipeline status in the GitLab UI.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need integrated CI/CD for GitLab-hosted projects with moderate build frequency and simple automation needs.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require unlimited build minutes, advanced enterprise features, or integration with non-GitLab repositories.
entry.body: GitLab CI/CD offers integrated pipelines for automating builds, tests, and deployments directly within GitLab, with a generous free tier for small teams and open source.

entry.title: Pulumi
entry.description: Pulumi is an infrastructure as code platform for provisioning and managing cloud resources using familiar programming languages.

freeTierDetails.summary: Pulumi offers a free Individual Edition for personal use and open source projects.

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a free Pulumi account.
Install the Pulumi CLI on your local machine.
Choose a supported programming language (e.g., TypeScript, Python, Go, C#).
Initialize a new Pulumi project using 'pulumi new'.
Configure your cloud provider credentials.
Run 'pulumi up' to provision your first stack.
entry.whenToUse: Use Pulumi when you want to manage cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages and need version control for your infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Pulumi if you require advanced team collaboration features or need enterprise-grade access controls in the free tier.
entry.body: Pulumi enables developers to define, deploy, and manage cloud infrastructure using real programming languages, supporting multiple clouds and offering a generous free tier for individuals.
